title: Tour P2 economics: absorption reservations + recovery-externality pricing — five engines coordinate the shared market depth (1ek0 P2 flagship)
description: Admiral-greenlit (in-chat 2026-07-10) as the 1ek0 P2 flagship. THE PROBLEM: continuous packed tours + arb-run + idle-arb legs + pre-positioning deposits all absorb the SAME market depth with zero cross-container coordination — the lbbm co-dump (-80k, bid 19,950→4) was this class; the lane mutex + flat 20min hold are the tactical patch. THE DESIGN GOAL: (a) ABSORPTION RESERVATIONS — a planner that schedules tranches against a market-good-side RESERVES that depth for its plan horizon, and other planners see remaining-unreserved depth (the A-cap generalized cross-plan); (b) RECOVERY-EXTERNALITY PRICING — dumping into a market prices the recovery time it imposes (the model's per-tier half-lives, 180-413min) against every engine's future legs, so the planner internalizes the crowding cost instead of the mutex externally blocking it. DESIGN PHASE FIRST (fable per RULINGS #9): where reservations live (daemon-side store vs solver input), staleness/expiry semantics, which engines consume (tour solver via snapshot; arb/idle-arb via dispatcher), interaction with the depth model + profit-primary + the D39 calibration, and what replaces/subsumes the lbbm mutex+hold. Design lands on THIS bead; Admiral rules on open questions; build lanes follow.
design:
# sp-78ai DESIGN — absorption reservations + recovery-externality pricing
Design-investigation pass (fable, RULINGS #9), 2026-07-10. NO code in this pass.
Every load-bearing claim verified in code; citations are file:line at HEAD (600ec5d).

## 0. The problem, verified

Five engines absorb the same market depth; the ONLY cross-engine signal is the market
cache, which is refreshed synchronously after every executed transaction
(cargo_transaction.go:305,443-463 — refreshMarketData → ScanAndSaveMarket overwrite).
So EXECUTED absorption is visible within seconds; what is invisible is:

- **Window 1 — in-flight absorption (plan→execution gap).** Engine A plans/dispatches
  against quoted depth; until A's trade lands and the cache refreshes, engine B quotes
  the same pre-absorption prices. This is exactly sp-lbbm (both hulls quoted the
  pre-crush 19,950 bid; −80k). Duration: minutes (arb leg) to hours (late legs of a
  6-hop tour).
- **Window 2 — post-execution recovery (the externality).** After A dumps, the cache
  truthfully shows the crushed price, so followers avoid the sink (self-correcting for
  AVOIDANCE). But (a) nothing tells the FIRST mover that its marginal tranche costs
  everyone a 3-7h recovery window (fitted half-lives: GROWING 180.7min / WEAK 279.1 /
  STRONG 386.1 / RESTRICTED 413.7 / untagged 1074.3 — model_artifacts/market_model.json
  `recovery`), and (b) the per-plan A-cap resets at every plan boundary: consecutive
  plans can each lawfully take 2 tranches at the same sink — the D39 ladder rebuilt
  ACROSS plans. The artifact's recovery half-lives are loaded (tour_handler.py:44-46)
  and consumed by NOTHING at decision time; the only recovery behavior in the fleet is
  the flat 20-min hold (idle_arb.go:144-151), whose own docstring concedes the model
  knows the real window and 20min deliberately understates it
  (idle_arb_lane_mutex.go:39-52).

Per-engine coordination status, verified:
- **Tours** (one-shot + continuous, Iterations=-1: run_tour_coordinator.go:99): the only
  depth-aware planner. Tranche prices decay per (supply|activity) tier per
  tradeVolume-sized tranche (tour_solver.py:96-125); pools are shared per
  (waypoint, good, side) across the whole tour ("depth is a property of the market, not
  of the leg index", tour_solver.py:299-302); A-cap = 2 planned tranches per
  (market, good, side) per tour (tour_solver.py:85,274-275), documented as "the interim
  stand-in for phase-2 recovery-externality pricing" (tour_solver.py:37-44). Sees
  nothing another container has planned.
- **Trade-route circuits**: scanLanes ranks cached spreads with no depth model and,
  confirmed under sp-q1ca, NO awareness of other circuits — two daemons "WILL rank
  identically and both select the same top lane"; resolution is emergent cache refresh
  or a bid-floor stop, "not the ranker itself" (run_trade_route_coordinator_lanes.go:29-46).
- **Arb-run one-shots**: quoted margin at dispatch, live re-verify fail-closed before
  buy, per-tranche sell floor at a fraction of the quoted bid — the H50 fix
  (run_arb_coordinator.go:15,43-60,347-358). Live truth only; no cross-container view.
- **Idle-arb legs**: uohe guard stack + the lbbm lane mutex — one hull per (good, sink)
  in flight + flat 20-min post-termination hold (idle_arb.go:537-583,
  idle_arb_lane_mutex.go:63-160). IN-MEMORY and per-dispatcher
  (idle_arb_lane_mutex.go:54-61): blocks only its own legs; a tour selling into a sink
  an idle-arb leg just crushed is invisible to it, and vice versa.
- **Pre-positioning deposits**: the deposit SIDE is a synthetic sink with no market
  impact, no decay, no A-cap (routing.proto:415-433, tour_solver.py:287-297) — nothing
  to reserve there. But the BUY side of a deposit leg absorbs real ask-side depth in a
  foreign market; it rides the tour plan's pools already, so it coordinates exactly as
  far as the tour does (i.e., not across containers).
- sp-2dv4's "absorption bound" (chain_margin_guard.go:30,146) is a single-container
  pre-spend projection — a within-plan bound like the A-cap, not coordination.

Decomposition the design follows: **reservations fix window 1; a decaying recovery
shadow fixes cross-plan laddering and re-entry timing; the honest externality "price"
is expressed in quantity space (occupied depth), not as an objective term** (§3).

## 1. Q1 — Reservation store + semantics

**Where: a daemon-side DB table (the w3he shape), not an in-memory coordinator, not
solver state.** The clinching argument is the EXECUTED shadow: a restart could
re-assert in-flight plans from recovered containers (they know their plans), but an
executed dump's recovery shadow is derivable from nothing after a restart — in-memory
loses it exactly when lbbm punished it. DB rows survive (RULINGS #2), and the
factory_spend_reservations machinery is the proven sibling: insert-then-check under a
per-player transaction-scoped advisory lock, release-on-every-exit, self-cleaning
stale sweep inside Reserve, no background job
(spend_reservation_repository.go:22,28,43,66-128,132-156; model at models.go:459-481;
wired at main.go:579; consumer discipline at production_executor.go:316-325).
The Python solver stays stateless per locked spec decision #5 — it NEVER reads the
store; Go assembles the netted view into the request (§2).

**One table, two row states** — `market_absorption_ledger` (name = build-lane detail):

| column | notes |
|---|---|
| id (uuid PK), player_id (idx), container_id, engine | engine ∈ tour/arb/idle-arb (telemetry + reclaim) |
| waypoint_symbol, good_symbol, side | composite idx with player_id — the outstanding query |
| state | PLANNED (in-flight intent) or EXECUTED (recovery shadow) |
| units | planned absorption / realized absorbed units |
| tier_at_write | supply\|activity stamped at write; readers resolve half-life from the artifact |
| quoted_price | telemetry only |
| created_at, executed_at, expires_at (idx) | lifecycle below |

**Lifecycle.**
- **PLANNED**: written at plan-accept (tours) or dispatch (arb/idle-arb). Released by
  the owner on every exit path (w3he defer discipline). Converted to EXECUTED per
  (waypoint,good,side) when the trades there complete, stamped with realized units +
  the live tier read the executor already performs (executeTrade's live re-verify).
  Expiry: per-plan TTL = 2× projected tour seconds + slack floor (flags, RULINGS #5) —
  a wedged container cannot hold depth past it. Dead-container reclaim: PLANNED rows
  whose container_id is no longer live in the container registry are swept inside
  every Reserve (the sp-vjwb orphan-reconcile precedent) — age alone cannot
  distinguish dead here (unlike w3he's 5-min window: healthy absorption reservations
  legitimately live the whole flight, w3he's live seconds).
- **EXECUTED**: never blocks by full units forever — readers decay it as
  units × 0.5^(elapsed/half_life(tier_at_write)) and treat the residual as occupied
  depth. expires_at = executed_at + hard cap (flag, default ~24h ≥ 2× the worst named
  tier); reads filter `expires_at > now`, physical deletion happens in Reserve's sweep
  (reads never write). No threshold for writing markers: units are recorded as-is and
  the tranche arithmetic (units/trade_volume) self-scales micro-trades to zero effect.
- **Reserve is CONDITIONAL (fail closed, RULINGS #4)**: inside the advisory-lock
  transaction — sweep, insert all rows for the plan, then check per (waypoint,good,side):
  decayed outstanding (all states, all containers) + this plan's units ≤
  fleet-wide cap = A-cap tranches × trade_volume. Any breach → roll back the WHOLE
  plan's rows (all-or-nothing) and report which sink failed. The tour coordinator
  treats a rejected reservation exactly like a degraded leg: stateless re-plan
  (machinery exists, run_tour_coordinator.go:503-531) against a fresh snapshot whose
  netting now shows the contested sink occupied. This closes the snapshot→accept
  TOCTOU the same way w3he closes check→buy. Lock key = (namespace "ABSB", player_id)
  — per-player serialization like w3he; per-row locks invite ordering deadlocks and
  Reserve calls are rare (per plan-accept/dispatch) and fast.
- **Era-scoping: NO era_id column.** These are ephemeral operational rows (the w3he
  "deliberately no FK" rationale, models.go:466-470). An era reset kills the
  containers → PLANNED rows are swept by the dead-container reclaim; EXECUTED rows
  age out on their hard cap and refer to (waypoint,good) keys whose quotes reset
  anyway. Era columns are for rows that persist weeks (waypoints/gate edges), not
  minutes-to-hours.
- **Restart (RULINGS #2)**: rows are DB state — they survive. Recovered containers
  still match their container_id and keep their holds; unrecovered containers' rows
  are swept. Strictly stronger than the lane mutex's documented in-memory concession
  (idle_arb_lane_mutex.go:54-61), which cannot protect the post-termination hold or
  anything cross-engine across a restart.

## 2. Q2 — Who writes, who reads, and the Go/Python boundary

**Writers (reserve + convert):**
- **Tour coordinator** (flagship): reserve at plan-accept — after the Feasible check
  at run_tour_coordinator.go:480, before executePlan at :503 — one PLANNED row per
  (waypoint,good,side) in the plan. On re-plan (:524): release all outstanding
  PLANNED rows for this container, reserve fresh from the new plan. Convert
  PLANNED→EXECUTED per sink as legs complete. Release-all on every exit path
  (deferred). Continuous circuits do this naturally per tour iteration.
- **Arb-run and idle-arb dispatchers**: reserve the sell side at launch — the exact
  seam where noteLaunch marks the mutex today (idle_arb.go:583) — units = full-hold
  worst case, TTL = 2× projected flight + slack. Release immediately if the launch
  fails post-reserve. The arb container converts to EXECUTED at sale completion with
  realized units + live tier (it already live-reads the market for the sell floor,
  run_arb_coordinator.go:347-358).
- **Deposits**: no writer — synthetic sink has no market depth (verified §0); the
  deposit's real BUY side is already inside the tour's plan rows.
- **Trade-route circuits: READ-ONLY in v1** (open question #1, §6). Circuits have no
  discrete bounded plan to reserve (reserving a circuit's future is unbounded), they
  move one hold per iteration, and the bid-floor stop bounds their loss class. Their
  executed trades already surface through the cache refresh.

**Readers (remaining-depth):**
- **Tour planner — via a new request field (the bc27/dchv additive-proto precedent),
  not Go-side snapshot mutation.** Mutating snapshot rows would have to lie (shrink
  trade_volume = wrong transaction size; pre-decay quotes = wrong prices). Instead:
  `repeated MarketAbsorption absorption = 6` on OptimizeTradeTourRequest
  (routing.proto:434-442): {waypoint_symbol, good_symbol, side, units_planned,
  units_recovering} — Go decays EXECUTED rows at assembly time so the solver stays
  clock-free and the recovery curve is consumed in exactly ONE place (the Go decay
  fn). Solver netting semantics (the price-honesty subtlety):
  - **units_planned** (in-flight, cache still pre-absorption): consume tranche
    capacity AND advance the price schedule — the plan's first tranche at that sink
    is priced at decay step ceil(units_planned/trade_volume), because those earlier
    tranches are being taken by someone at those prices.
  - **units_recovering** (executed, cache already shows the crushed quote): consume
    tranche CAPACITY ONLY — pricing still starts at the live quote's step 0, or the
    damage would be double-counted (quote already reflects it).
  Mechanically this is _TranchePool initialization + the cap arithmetic in pool()
  (tour_solver.py:128-148,264-278); the A-cap budget now counts pre-consumed tranches,
  which makes the cap FLEET-WIDE-outstanding instead of per-plan — the A-cap
  generalized cross-plan, exactly the bead's design goal (a).
- **Idle-arb dispatcher**: one batched outstanding query per pass →
  pickHubLocalLane consults the map — skip:reserved (new reason alongside
  skipReasonLaneHeld, idle_arb.go:387-394) when planned outstanding > 0 on the
  (good,sink) or the decayed recovering residual ≥ the shadow floor (knob, §6). v1 is
  binary skip, not quote-adjustment: the leg's own live-verify + relative floor +
  sell floor already own price truth, and this keeps the dispatcher loop pure — note
  the mutex docstring's objection (idle_arb_lane_mutex.go:44-46) was to coupling the
  dispatcher to the ROUTING SERVICE (an RPC in the decision loop); loading the
  repo-checked artifact JSON once at boot into a pure decay function is a different
  weight class entirely.
- **Trade-route scanLanes**: read-only — subtract decayed outstanding from each
  lane's effective depth in ranking and skip sinks under a heavy shadow, replacing
  "discover the collision by margin-death" (run_trade_route_coordinator_lanes.go:44-46)
  with avoid-by-knowledge.

**Go-side model access**: Go loads the SAME market_model.json at daemon boot (path
flag, RULINGS #5; version logged; fit pipeline and artifact untouched). Artifact
unreadable Go-side → decay cannot be computed → EXECUTED residuals are treated as
UNDECAYED until their hard-cap expiry (fail closed on reserved depth per RULINGS #4)
while trading on unreserved depth continues unhindered — reconciling #4 with the
spec's "trading is never blocked on the planner" posture.

## 3. Q3 — Recovery-externality pricing: the honest form

The marginal cost a dump imposes on OTHERS is the profit that depth would have earned
during the recovery it triggers. Computing that exactly requires future demand at the
sink — unknowable. The three candidate forms:

(a) **Objective term** (profit − externality): requires a utilization factor λ (how
much of the recovery window others would actually have used) — a made-up knob whose
error silently reshapes tour selection. It also re-introduces a shaped objective
through the side door after the Admiral explicitly ruled profit-primary against
rate-shaping (tour_solver.py:27-35, spec §Solve-4 revision), and it would need its own
reporting split to keep plan-vs-realized telemetry honest.

(b) **Reduced-available-depth with half-life decay** (the EXECUTED shadow of §1):
prices the externality in QUANTITY space — the absorber doesn't pay a synthetic tax;
instead nobody (including its own next plan) can step into the hole until the model
says it has regrown. Enforceable, fail-closed, and it is precisely "the cap can relax
to the economics" promised in tour_solver.py:41-44: fleet-wide outstanding absorption
at any sink is bounded at A-cap tranches, decaying on the fitted curve.

**Recommendation: (b) now; (a) as a REPORTED shadow-price only.** Every plan logs
`projected_recovery_burden` (per sink: units × half_life(tier), summed) next to
profit/cph — the Admiral gets the externality made visible in telemetry, selection
stays profit-primary, and if burn-in evidence ever shows (b) leaving value on the
table, (a) can graduate from reported to selecting the same way cph earned its
tiebreak seat. D39 calibration untouched: no fitted coefficient changes, no change to
how impact curves price the tranches a plan DOES take (§2's double-count rule keeps
price math exact); only tranche AVAILABILITY is consulted differently.

## 4. Q4 — What subsumes lbbm's mutex + flat hold; what stays

Retires (lane 5, after burn-in evidence):
- **The flat 20-min hold** → replaced by the EXECUTED shadow decaying on fitted
  half-lives (a RESTRICTED sink stays occupied ~hours, honestly, instead of 20min).
- **The in-flight (good,sink) mutex** → subsumed by PLANNED reservations: cross-ENGINE
  now, not just cross-pass within one dispatcher; within-pass dedupe property is
  preserved because the reservation is written synchronously at dispatch (same seam,
  idle_arb.go:583). Restart posture strictly improves (§1).
  Rollout: mutex runs in parallel with store consultation for one observation window
  (flag-gated, RULINGS #5), then idle_arb_lane_mutex.go is deleted.

Stays, untouched (RULINGS #4 — layered defense, live truth outranks any ledger):
- **Per-tranche sell floor** (run_arb_coordinator.go:347-358) — the last line when
  reservations are wrong/late; live truth beats the model.
- **Live re-verify + relative margin floor** (idle_arb.go:414-421; tour tolerance
  check) and every money guard beneath them.
- **The per-plan A-cap** stays in v1: the fleet-wide cap equals it only when the store
  is consulted and healthy; the per-plan form is the proven local guard and costs
  nothing. Its RELAXATION (to >2 tranches when the shadow says depth exists) is
  deliberately the LAST lane, gated on the Admiral's evidence standard (§6).

## 5. Q5 — Build lanes (ordered, independently gateable)

**L1 — absorption ledger + Go decay library (substrate).** New
persistence/absorption_ledger repository + model (+ AutoMigrate at models.go:610
idiom), domain port, artifact loader + decay fn (services), flags. Template:
spend_reservation_repository{,_test}.go. Acceptance: CRUD/decay/sweep/conditional-
reserve tests incl. two-writer serialization + restart-survival. Deploy: daemon only.
Tier: opus. Risk: LOW (proven pattern).

**L2 — arb/idle-arb consult + write (kills the lbbm class cross-engine early, no
proto).** idle_arb.go (batched read per pass, skip:reserved, reserve-at-launch,
release-on-failed-launch), arb container converts to EXECUTED at sale. Mutex stays
armed in parallel. Acceptance: two-dispatcher collision sim → second skips:reserved;
existing lbbm tests green. Deploy: daemon. Tier: opus. Risk: MEDIUM.

**L3 — tour writer + proto field + solver netting (flagship; THE RISKIEST).**
routing.proto MarketAbsorption + regen; tour_solver.py pool init + cap arithmetic +
golden tests (planned advances price schedule, recovering consumes capacity only);
tour_handler.py conversion; grpc_routing_client.go + mocks; run_tour_coordinator.go
reserve/convert/release at :480/:524/executeTrade/exit paths; assembly-time decay in
plan() (:871-898). Acceptance: golden plan-around-occupied-sink; reserve-reject →
re-plan; death-sweep; version-skew safe (additive field — old solver ignores it,
behaves as today). Deploy: daemon AND routing service (the one coordinated deploy).
Tier: fable (three-language surface + lifecycle/concurrency edges). Risk: HIGH.

**L4 — trade-route read-only consult.** scanLanes ranking subtracts decayed
outstanding + shadow-skip, verdict-log parity. Acceptance: shadowed lane ranks below
clean smaller-spread lane. Deploy: daemon. Tier: sonnet/opus. Risk: LOW-MEDIUM.

**L5 — retirement + relaxation memo.** After burn-in (skip:reserved counters live,
zero cross-engine co-dumps over the window): delete laneMutex + flat hold; memo to
the Admiral with telemetry on whether/when the per-plan A-cap relaxes. Tier: sonnet.
Risk: LOW.

Order: L1 → (L2 ∥ L4) → L3 → L5. Only L3 touches proto/routing deploys.

## 6. Q6 — Open questions for the Admiral

1. **Trade-route circuits: read-only v1 (recommended) or full reserve-writers?**
   Writing means inventing a plan horizon for an engine that has none and touching the
   oldest money path; the residual circuit-vs-circuit race is the documented sp-q1ca
   class with a one-hold bound. Join later if telemetry shows circuit-vs-tour
   collisions.
2. **Shadow aggressiveness knobs** (economics, not engineering): the floor fraction at
   which a recovering shadow stops blocking (proposal: 50% of one tranche), and the
   EXECUTED hard-cap lifetime (proposal: 24h). Both flags (RULINGS #5).
3. **Externality objective term**: confirm REPORT-ONLY (projected_recovery_burden
   logged per plan, never selecting) — or name the telemetry bar at which a
   shadow-priced objective experiment is warranted.
4. **A-cap relaxation standard**: what evidence relaxes the per-plan 2-tranche cap
   (the tour_solver.py:41-44 promise)? Proposal: only after L5 telemetry shows the cap
   binding on a meaningful share of profitable plans AND zero cross-plan ladder
   incidents over the window.

## 7. RULINGS compliance

#2 store is DB-backed, restart-survivable, dead-container-swept (w3he/vjwb idioms).
#3 daemon-only writers; Python stays stateless/read-nothing. #4 conditional Reserve
fails closed; artifact-unreadable degrades to MORE conservative blocking, never less;
no existing guard weakened (sell floor/live-verify/A-cap all stay). #5 every knob a
flag (TTL slack, shadow floor, hard cap, consult gates, artifact path). #9 this pass
is the deliberate economics design; lane tiers assigned. D39: fit pipeline, artifact,
and per-tranche price math untouched — only tranche availability is consulted
differently, with the planned-vs-recovering split preventing price double-counting.
notes:
ADMIRAL DELEGATION (2026-07-10 ~17:45Z, in-chat): the four §6 open rulings are delegated to the TRADE ANALYST — request mailed st-wisp-d8kh with per-item RULE format. Analyst's word is the ruling; L1 (ledger) + L2 (arb consult) dispatch on their reply.
TRADE-ANALYST RULINGS (sp-78ai §6, Admiral-delegated 17:37Z; per the delegation, these ARE the rulings) — 2026-07-10 ~17:50Z

**Q1 — Trade-route circuits in v1: RULE: READ-ONLY.** A reservation needs a plan horizon to expire against; circuits have none, so their "reservations" would be either lies or leaks in the ledger, on the oldest money path. The residual circuit-vs-circuit race is documented and one-hold-bounded (sp-q1ca). JOIN-LATER TRIGGER (named now so nobody re-litigates): circuits become writers when L5 telemetry shows circuit-into-tour-reserved-sink collisions at >2 incidents/day OR any single collision with ≥50k realized loss. Until then, L4's read-only consult (shadow-skip in scanLanes) captures ~all the value at ~none of the risk.

**Q2 — Shadow aggressiveness knobs: RULE: (a) ACCEPT 50%-of-one-tranche floor; (b) REJECT 24h — set EXECUTED hard-cap to 12h, and EXCLUDE untagged-tier markets from shadow-writing.**
(a) At 50% of a tranche recovered, a new sell takes the recovered half-tranche near full price with the bp6f 80%-of-quote floor already armed downstream — earlier unblocking rebuilds ladders, later blocking strands capital; 50% is the balanced point and it composes with the per-tier decay fn (fraction of depth, not wall-clock).
(b) 24h is more than half the remaining era (~45h) — a stuck shadow would embargo a sink for most of the rest of the game. The slowest TAGGED tier half-life is 413.7min (~6.9h); 12h ≈ 2 full half-lives of the slowest tagged tier and ~4x the median, and with 29 probe posts re-reading hourly the cap should almost never be the thing that clears a shadow. Untagged markets (1074min ≈ 18h half-life) get NO shadows: any shadow there is either wrong or effectively eternal, and we have no business bulk-dumping into unmodeled markets — the depth model can't price what it hasn't fit.

**Q3 — Externality objective term: RULE: CONFIRMED REPORT-ONLY for the remainder of this era (profit-primary ruling C stands). The experiment bar, named:** log projected_recovery_burden per plan starting at L3; the live shadow-priced objective becomes warranted only when BOTH: (i) report-only logs show ≥20% of plans selecting a sink whose projected recovery burden EXCEEDS that plan's own margin at that sink (i.e., measurably self-harming choices), AND (ii) an offline replay of ≥48h of logged plans with the term enabled shows ≥5% fleet $/hr improvement vs realized. Replay first, live second — the objective function of a 1.6-2M/hr engine does not get A/B-tested in production on a hunch.

**Q4 — A-cap relaxation standard: RULE: ACCEPT with numbers and two additions.** Relax 2→3 tranches (ONE step at a time, per-tier) only when, over a rolling 24h window: (a) the cap binds on ≥15% of profitable plans (truncation events where the capped marginal tranche projected ≥600/u — the bp6f floor); (b) ZERO cross-plan ladder incidents (same market-good-side absorbing ≥3 tranches within one tier half-life across consecutive plans — L5's counter); AND (c) the reservation ledger is LIVE for tours (L1-L3 deployed). Addition (c) is load-bearing: the A-cap is the interim stand-in FOR the ledger (tour_solver.py:41-44's own promise) — relaxing it before the replacement exists reintroduces the D39 ladder class with a bigger fleet. Addition: relaxation applies to GROWING/STRONG tiers only (180-386min recovery); RESTRICTED and untagged never relax this era.

**Cross-cutting note for the lanes:** L1+L2 are unblocked by these rulings immediately; L3's values (Q2 knobs) matter mostly beyond this era's ~45h — do not let flag-tuning delay the L2 deploy that kills the live lbbm class today. My in-flight-exposure finding (sp-i8vx) is indeed the same structural hole this design closes from the market side; the two should reference each other at build time.

L4 (trade-route read-only consult) queues on L1's landing; L3 (tour solver integration, fable-designed values) after; L5 (retire mutex + telemetry counters incl. the Q4 relaxation evidence) last.

title: P2: contract worker calls FULFILL before deliveries register — 'failed to fulfill contract: deliveries not complete' ×2 on TORWIND-24 (deliver→fulfill sequencing race; coordinator retry self-heals but burns cycles)
description: 05:25Z+: contract-work-TORWIND-24 crashed twice with 'failed to fulfill: deliveries not complete' — the worker fires fulfill immediately after deliver without confirming the delivery registered (or fulfills after a partial multi-trip delivery). Coordinator respawns and the retry completes (contracts still flowing), so impact = wasted cycles + crash noise, not stuck contracts. title: P1 (Admiral: pace): tour-run --iterations -1 — CONTINUOUS touring (tour completes → re-plan from current position → fly again, no captain in the loop) + handle pre-held cargo in planning
description: Admiral verdict 05:05Z: 'this is fucking too slow'. Root cause: every tour/circuit is ONE-SHOT — completion returns the hull to captain-paced relaunch (wake cadence ≈ 10-15min dead time per cycle, worse when laden exits need manual sells). The heavy lost ~3h tonight to wake-paced shepherding. FIX: (1) --iterations -1 on tour-run: on manifest completion, re-plan from the CURRENT position/market state and fly the next tour immediately — margin-exit/starvation-exit semantics like trade-route, lifecycle contract applies (finish-leg, honest exits); (2) planner treats PRE-HELD cargo as manifest input (sell legs for existing inventory first) — kills the laden-exit→manual-rescue class entirely; (3) then the heavy runs: one launch at era-scale caps, earns until margins die. This is THE pace multiplier: engine-cadence capital velocity instead of captain-cadence.

title: AD-HOC ARB RUN command: one-shot, TARGETED, GUARDED arbitrage (buy G@source → sell@dest, capped, once) — the missing middle between manual-flying and the unguarded daemon
description:
Admiral gap (2026-07-09): captain is hand-flying arb legs (position→buy→jump→sell) because the ONLY automation is all-or-nothing — 'workflow trade-route' is AUTONOMOUS (auto-picks lane, can't target) + CONTINUOUS (loops) + UNGUARDED (no spend floor; drained 11M→43k). There is no way to say 'fly THIS hull on THIS lane, ONCE, safely.' Every deliberate arb becomes fiddly manual driving that hits fuel-routing, ku8e claim-races, and in-system-only navigate.

BUILD: 'spacetraders workflow arb-run' (or 'ship arb') — one-shot, captain-specified lane, guarded. FLAGS: --ship, --good, --buy-at <waypoint>, --sell-at <waypoint> (may be cross-system → auto gate+jump+refuel routing), --max-units (cap to sink volume), --max-spend (working-capital cap), --min-margin (per-unit floor). BEHAVIOR: re-read LIVE prices at buy time; ABORT if realized margin < --min-margin OR spend would exceed --max-spend OR actual location ≠ intended; buy ≤max-units at source; route (cross-gate w/ refuel) to dest; sell; RELEASE hull (one-shot, no loop). Reuses the daemon's existing cross-gate travel() + the bp6f guard logic, just captain-directed + single-shot.

WHY P1: directly enables what the Admiral is asking for NOW (safe captain-directed arb) WITHOUT waiting for the full autonomous bp6f daemon, and WITHOUT the manual-driving friction. It's the fastest path to profitable trade resuming. ACCEPTANCE: one command flies a hull buy@A → (jump) → sell@B with a spend cap + margin floor, aborts cleanly on bad price/insufficient funds, releases the hull after one loop.

The p4ua gap is ONLY the trade sequencing + guards (buy→jump→sell with floor + tranche caps, one shot) — fuel management already exists in the routing layer it will reuse. title: Trade-route 10x levers: add lane-targeting override + tune the -200/unit cross-system penalty (cross-gate EXECUTION confirmed working)
description: THE multi-system-trading blocker. sp-wlev ('scan + fly gate-crossing circuits') is CLOSED, but captain's empirical test (15:0xZ): launching 'trade-route --system X1-GQ92' on a home hull did NOT relocate it cross-gate — exited fast, single-system behavior. Flag doc confirms '--system = System to scan (required)' SINGULAR. So the deployed router does single-system arbitrage requiring the hull to already BE in the target system; it does NOT (a) scan the connected graph, (b) relocate hulls cross-gate, or (c) run buy-in-A / jump / sell-in-B circuits. Either wlev closed with less than its title, or there's a usage/config path I'm missing. JOINT-FORCE ITEM #1. Need from shipwright: does autonomous cross-gate work at all, and if not, what's the exact gap + build path? Acceptance: a hull at home, given a target rich system, autonomously buys home-exportable goods, jumps to the rich sink, sells, returns — margin logged per leg. Ties: sp-pnx0 (hold-vs-absorption weighting), sp-l7h2/i1pm (stop poaching so distributed hulls hold position).
notes: DEPLOYED pid 57322 (batch w/ lgnh). Autonomous amortized penalty live — watch for pen= tokens in m= lane-selection lines on undirected scans.
close_reason: Captain WRITTEN ACCEPT (st-wisp-c87l): autonomous cross-gate legs WON undirected scans post-deploy — heavy flew them unprompted, pen= tokens visible in m= lines. The time-opportunity-cost penalty (423e284) delivering exactly the frontier unlock it was built for. Both halves of this bead now accepted (--dest targeting earlier; autonomous selection now).

title: Watchkeeper wake-throttle: session cap consumed by failed/spurious wakes starves the captain (18-24 events perpetually queued at 8/h)
description: Why: captain woken <=8/h with 18-24 events perpetually queued (409 'session cap reached' log lines) — starved, not driving the fleet (Admiral-visible). Root causes in supervisor.go Tick(): (1) rememberAttempt (line 251) counts the wake against the cap BEFORE bridgeWake (252), so a FAILED/timed-out delivery still burns cap budget without draining events — and API 'context deadline exceeded' failures are rife right now. (2) false-liveness flapping (sp-0d1g: logs 'STANDING SESSION DOWN captain' while captain alive) muddies wake decisions. (3) default MaxSessionsPerHour=6 (defaults.go:142), config override 8 — too low for an active fleet (poll every 30s = 120 polls/h). INTERIM applied 2026-07-09: raised gitignored gobot/config.yaml max_sessions_per_hour 8->20 + restarted watchkeeper. What: (a) count only SUCCESSFUL wake deliveries against the cap (move rememberAttempt after a successful bridgeWake); (b) raise the default; (c) ensure sp-0d1g flapping doesn't create spurious wake pressure; (d) confirm queued events are real, not duplicates (dedup if needed).
acceptance_criteria: Captain woken as often as real fleet events warrant (queue drains, not perpetually 18-24 deep); failed wake deliveries do NOT consume the session cap.
notes: session died
close_reason: LIVE 5b61f9e (watchkeeper deployed pid 9962, daemon untouched). Root fix: recordWake split so ONLY firstWake charges the hourly session cap — re-nudges/heartbeats no longer consume it, new-event wakes not starved, runaway protection preserved. 4 new tests + 1 updated, internal/captain suite green. Supersedes the 8->20 config stopgap.

title: Gate hardening: catch real-API-contract mismatches pre-deploy (payload vs OpenAPI, handler-registration completeness, real error-shape tests)
description: Why: every daemon regression this era passed the captain-gate (compile + unit tests vs fakes) and only broke in the captain's LIVE acceptance, so the production fleet became the test environment. Examples: sp-n0x7 shipped an unregistered handler + wrong JSON key (systemSymbol vs waypointSymbol); factory crashes came from unmodeled real-API error responses. What: add pre-deploy checks to the gate/test suite: (1) validate every outbound SpaceTraders request payload against the published OpenAPI spec; (2) a handler-registration completeness check (every command handler actually wired in main.go); (3) error-path tests using the REAL API error response shapes.
acceptance_criteria: Gate fails a deliberately mis-keyed payload AND a deliberately-unregistered handler; error-path tests exercise real API error shapes.
close_reason: Gate hardening deployed live (daemon 46019): handler-registration completeness + real-error-shape tests + isAlreadyInOrbitError fix.

title: REMOVE the rollover/handoff mechanism entirely — Admiral directive; it caused a 4h income collapse and is obsolete on 1M context
description: ADMIRAL DIRECT ORDER (2026-07-09): rip out the rollover mechanism from EVERYWHERE. WHY: the rollover-due nudge (fires ~40-53h) tells the captain to run 'gc handoff', which requests a restart — but there is NO auto-respawn in this environment (watchkeeper alerts, does not respawn). Result: the LIVE session drops into 'minimal mode / await restart' limbo with no successor ever coming, goes passive exactly when the fleet needs active management. On 2026-07-09 this contributed to a 4h collapse where the contract engine died undetected. Obsolete now anyway: captain runs on Opus-4.8 1M-context, so the weekly-quota/context-growth reason for rollover is gone. REMOVE FROM EVERYWHERE: (1) the rollover-due nudge emitter in the supervisor/watchkeeper; (2) the Rollover section + rollover-nudge handling in the captain prompt template (agents/captain/prompt.template.md) AND every crew template (shipwright/trade-analyst/fleet-architect/surveyor) that references rollover/gc-handoff/minimal-mode-await-restart; (3) any wake-ritual step that says 'first act is rollover'. If a manual context reset is ever wanted it should be an explicit Admiral action, never an automatic nudge that cripples the live session. ACCEPTANCE: no rollover-due nudge ever fires; no template instructs gc handoff / minimal-mode-await-restart; grep for 'rollover'/'gc handoff' across templates+supervisor returns only historical notes.
notes:
session died
session died
DEPLOYED live 2026-07-09 11:34 (watchkeeper rebuilt via make build-watchkeeper + kickstart com.spacetraders.captain; pid 45015, running 6f19638, last exit 0, no panic, clean startup — VERIFIED). Rollover fully removed: nudge emitter deleted, ListSessions dead-code removed, Rollover sections stripped from all 5 crew templates (verified via gc prime --strict render path), config RolloverNudgeHours removed. Main daemon NOT restarted (sp-s8qq doesn't touch it — spared the factory-relaunch drill). Templates take effect on next crew spawn; running crew keep in-context templates until respawn but get ZERO rollover nudges now. Removes the 4h-collapse cause. Revert=git revert 6f19638 + rebuild watchkeeper.
close_reason: Deployed live 6f19638 (watchkeeper verified healthy pid 45015); rollover mechanism removed — resolves the 4h-collapse cause.

title: income.stalled detector is aggregate-blind — a single-engine collapse (contracts dead 4h) goes undetected when another engine masks it
description: ROOT CAUSE of the 2026-07-09 4h income collapse going undetected: contract engine flatlined (42k/4h vs ~1.6M expected) but income.stalled NEVER fired because factories kept aggregate TRADING_REVENUE flowing (+1.13M/4h). The detector watches total income, not per-line. FIX: per-engine stall detection — separate income.stalled signals for CONTRACT_REVENUE, TRADING_REVENUE (trade circuits), and mfg/construction; fire if ANY established earning line drops >X% below its trailing rate for >N min, even if aggregate is healthy. This is the safety net that failed — without it, any single engine can die silently behind the others. ACCEPTANCE: kill the contract coordinator with factories running → a contract-line stall event fires within one detection window.
notes:
session died
session died
close_reason: Deployed live (watchkeeper e838149); captain confirms per-engine stall detection passive+live.

title: Trade-route ranker should weight hull-hold vs lane absorption — a 225-cargo heavy on a vol-20 lane earns like a light
description: Captain flag (st-wisp-t0i4, 2026-07-09): TORWIND-19 (225-cargo heavy) landed on a low-vol (~20) lane. If the lane ranker (sp-wlev scanLanes/rankLanes) doesn't weight the ship's HOLD against the lane's ABSORPTION/volume, a big hull on a low-absorption lane can't fill its hold and earns like a small hull — wasting the heavy. Ranker/assignment should prefer high-absorption lanes for big hulls (and small hulls for thin lanes), or factor hold-utilization into the per-lane value. Relates to sp-snmb hull right-sizing but is the LANE-side dimension.
notes: ELEVATED TO P1 (shipwright-confirmed 15:4xZ as a real 10x blocker): ranking uses lane absorption only (min source/dest volume); ship hold NEVER enters ranking → 225-cargo heavy ranked onto a shallow lane, crushes it after 1 visit (TORWIND-19 autopsy). This + lane-targeting (sp-xwa1) are THE two multi-system-trading blockers per code recon. Cross-gate execution already works.
close_reason: LIVE 8f15201 (rode with bp6f) — hull-hold-vs-lane weighting folded into unified reranker.

title: special/high-value hulls (heavy freighter) get poached by mfg pool the instant a circuit ends — need role-exclusion like the static contract fleet
description: Our 2.07M HEAVY_FREIGHTER TORWIND-19 (bulk-circuit earmark, sp-n9vb) was claimed by the manufacturing coordinator the instant its trade-route circuit exited — flew an EMPTY 225-hold mfg feed leg, capex advantage fully wasted, until captain caught it and reserved it manually. Same root as sp-snmb element-5 (static dedicated fleet): high-value/special-role hulls must be EXCLUDED from general-pool claiming. FIX: a hull-role/dedication registry (config: bulk_circuit_fleet = [TORWIND-19], contract_fleet = [...], excavator_pool = [...]) where the general mfg/factory coordinators skip any hull not in their pool. Captain reservation is the manual workaround but it's fragile (doesn't survive restarts, blocks captain's own CLI verbs per sp-sfoe). Ties together sp-snmb (contract dedication), this (bulk dedication), and the reservation bugs. ACCEPTANCE: a hull flagged to a dedicated pool is never claimed by another coordinator; survives daemon restart; captain can still direct it.
notes:
SAME pattern as sp-snmb (st-wisp-ev78): special/dedicated hulls (e.g. the trade-route heavy TORWIND-19) get POACHED by mfg the instant their circuit ends — need POOL-EXCLUSION (coordinator-exclusive claim-filter), exactly what sp-snmb builds for the contract fleet. Can WAIT (captain reserved TORWIND-19, protected). Build by GENERALIZING sp-snmb's claim-filter to any coordinator's dedicated hulls, once sp-snmb lands.
Resolved by sp-l7h2: the persistent ship-to-operation dedication primitive (fleet assign/unassign CLI, DedicatedFleet DB column, ClaimShip atomic enforcement) generalizes exactly the fix this issue asks for — a hull-role/dedication registry where any coordinator's dedicated hulls are excluded from other coordinators' claims, survives daemon restart (see TestDedicatedFleet_PersistsAcrossSaveAndFreshRepositoryReload), and is captain-settable via 'fleet assign TORWIND-19 bulk_circuit'. sp-l7h2 subsumes this issue entirely — same root cause, same fix, built once. Leaving OPEN for team-lead to close at deploy.
close_reason: Resolved by sp-l7h2 (ownership/anti-poach) + sp-bi75 (pins persist across restart, verified). Heavy no longer poached when pinned to 'trade'.

title: goods factory crashes on 'no cargo space available for purchase' — buys inputs without checking hull free space (distinct from vsfn refuel-500)
description: NEW crash signature 06:0xZ (goods_factory-SHIP_PARTS-ff2078ee): 'parallel production failed: level 0 execution failed: no cargo space available for purchase'. Distinct from sp-vsfn (refuel-500). Here the coordinator attempted an input BUY while the delivery hull already held cargo it had not yet dropped — no free-space precondition. Same terminal-crash-not-park failure mode as vsfn (loses chain state, needs captain relaunch). This + vsfn + npyr-dwell are the 3 factory failure modes draining the duty-cycle line tonight (6+ factory deaths). FIX: check available cargo space before issuing a purchase task; if full, sequence a delivery/sell first; park+resume on the edge case rather than crash. ACCEPTANCE: factory with a partially-loaded hull never crashes on purchase; injected full-hull state triggers deliver-then-buy, not crash.
notes:
DOMINANT KILLER (06:2xZ): this signature took BOTH SHIP_PARTS and SHIP_PLATING this wake (crashes 2317 + 2350) — it is now the #1 factory-death cause, ahead of vsfn's refuel-500. Bumped P1. MEDICINE staying PARKED (weakest margin per analyst) to cut relaunch churn until this + vsfn land; running ADV_CIRC + PARTS + PLATING (3 strong-margin factories). At current rate factories die ~40min after launch — each death = ~40min lost production + captain relaunch. Fix cannot wait behind wlev.
DEDUP confirmed: identical crash to sp-mu6u (same crash string 'no cargo space available for purchase', same code path buyGood in production_executor.go, same cited instance SHIP_PARTS-ff2078ee appears in both bead reports). sp-mu6u merged as 73ec39b (now on main): buyGood detects availableSpace<=0, calls new freeCargoSpace() to best-effort sell onboard cargo at the current docked market, retries the purchase if space freed, else skips gracefully (0-unit result, WARN log, no crash). Verified via grep: the literal string 'no cargo space available for purchase' no longer exists anywhere in production_executor.go on main. Acceptance criteria satisfied: a partially/fully-loaded hull never crashes on input purchase; full-hull triggers sell-then-buy, not crash. INPUT PATH: fully covered, no rework needed. RESIDUAL: sibling function purchaseFabricatedOutput (the OUTPUT-harvest path) has an analogous unfixed crash - 'no cargo space available for output' at production_executor.go:544 - same root cause, not yet fixed by 73ec39b (out of scope for sp-mu6u). Proceeding to fix this residual now in a fresh worktree, applying the same freeCargoSpace pattern, per team-lead direction.
DEPLOYED live 2026-07-09 08:00 (daemon pid 66906, main@91785c4, SINGLE-bead deploy under new one-at-a-time verified-deploy discipline). Fix: purchaseFabricatedOutput 'no cargo space available for output' crash — now frees hold at the docked market (freeCargoSpace, same guard as sp-mu6u input path) then harvests, or skips gracefully (0 units, nil err) if nothing sells. Input path already covered by sp-mu6u/73ec39b (verified, NOT rebuilt). Merged 91785c4: production_executor.go +25/-1, new output_full_hold_test.go +95; TDD RED->GREEN; full suite green. Health post-restart: daemon running, contract + manufacturing engines active, no panic. CAPTAIN ACCEPTANCE SIGNAL: zero 'no cargo space available for output' crashes in factory/manufacturing containers going forward (notably via collectExistingFactorySupply harvesting with residue onboard). Recurrence -> flag me; revert = git revert 91785c4 + redeploy.
close_reason: Deployed live 91785c4 (single-bead, verified deploy), daemon healthy, captain given acceptance signal + revert path; watching for output-path crash recurrence.

title: Generalize static hull earmark: persistent ship→operation binding (contract_fleet + bulk_circuit_fleet) so mfg/trade can't poach dedicated hulls
description: Root primitive behind sp-snmb element 5 AND the heavy-freighter earmark — build ONCE, two consumers. EVIDENCE (05:1x-06:0xZ): TORWIND-19 (2.07M heavy freighter, bulk-circuit earmark per sp-n9vb) finished its trade-route circuit, went idle, and the MFG COORDINATOR immediately poached it for a mfg-task (mfg-task-TORWIND-19-40d2ffbe) — a 225-hold now hauling ~40u of factory input, the exact 'earns like a light' waste the earmark exists to prevent. Reservation doesn't solve it (reserve blocks trade-route CLI, sp-sfoe; and reservations don't survive daemon restarts). MECHANISM: a persistent hull-tag / dedicated-fleet config, e.g.  and , where tagged hulls are claimable ONLY by their owning operation's coordinator — same static-dedication the Admiral ruled for contracts (captain-prefer-static-assignment memory), generalized so it survives restarts and needs no arbitration. sp-snmb's contract_fleet is one consumer; the heavy's bulk_circuit is another. ACCEPTANCE: tag a hull to an operation; other coordinators never claim it (verify mfg cannot grab a bulk_circuit hull); tags persist across daemon restart; captain CLI can read/set tags.
design:
# Fleet-Allocation Ownership Architecture — Design (for Admiral/captain review)

Status: DESIGN ONLY, no code written. Author: harbormaster (shipwright rig).
Rev 2: converges sp-snmb's shipped mechanism into this design (not a parallel
one) and locks the three open questions from rev 1 as decisions.

## 1. Root cause, restated precisely from code (not just symptoms)

The fleet has **three independent "claim a ship" code paths today**, only one
of which is atomic, and **one dedication concept that only one of five
coordinators knows about**:

| Coordinator | How it claims a ship | Atomic (DB-locked)? | Dedication-aware? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Manufacturing | `ShipRepository.ClaimShip` (`SELECT ... FOR UPDATE` tx) | Yes | Discovery only (via `FindIdleLightHaulers`) |
| Factory | `claimShipForFactory`: in-memory `ship.IsIdle()` check → `AssignToContainer` → `shipRepo.Save` | **No** — classic read-modify-write race | Discovery only (same `FindIdleLightHaulers` call) |
| Contract | `ClaimShip` (fleet coordinator) + `FindIdleDedicatedShips` for its own pool | Yes | **Read+write** — the only coordinator that sets `DedicatedFleet` |
| Trade-route | Direct `shipRepo.FindBySymbol` → mutate → `shipRepo.Save` | **No** — same race as factory | **None** — doesn't call `FindIdleLightHaulers` at all |
| Gas | Same pattern as trade-route | **No** | **None** |

Two distinct bugs are being conflated by the "poaching" symptom, and the
design has to fix both:

- **Bug A — missing tags, not a race**: `task_assignment_manager.go` already
  calls `contract.FindIdleLightHaulers`, which already excludes any ship with
  `DedicatedFleet() != ""`. TORWIND-19 was poachable because *no fleet ever
  tagged it* — `bulk_circuit`/trade-route/gas dedication doesn't exist as a
  concept yet, only `contract` does, and only when the operator remembers to
  pass `--dedicated-ships`. This is a **coverage gap**, fixed by generalizing
  the tag + a real assign/unassign command (sections 4, 7).
- **Bug B — the real race**: `dedicated_fleet` is checked in exactly one
  function (`FindIdleLightHaulers`), which is a **discovery** read. The
  **claim/write** path (`ClaimShip`) never checks it. Any caller that skips
  discovery — or any of the two non-atomic claim paths above — can grab a
  dedicated hull with zero resistance. This is a **structural enforcement
  gap**, fixed by moving the check into the one atomic choke point (section 4).

Fixing only the tag-coverage gap (giving trade-route/gas/factory a fleet name)
without fixing the enforcement gap leaves the TOCTOU exactly as exploitable as
today — it would just take a different bug to trigger it. Both are in scope.

## 2. Design principle (binding constraint)

Per Admiral doctrine already on record: **prefer static dedicated assignment
over dynamic claim arbitration**. This design does not introduce priorities,
tethers, or a scoring/arbitration engine. It generalizes the one static
primitive that already shipped (`Ship.DedicatedFleet()`), and it accepts idle
time on a dedicated hull as a price, not a defect, when the owning line's
throughput matters more than fleet-wide utilization.

## 3. sp-snmb → general convergence (read this before section 4)

sp-snmb already shipped a **partial, contract-only** version of this exact
mechanism. Nothing below is a parallel system — every shipped piece folds
into the general one, and Phase 1 (section 9) is scoped specifically to make
that true in code, not just on paper.

| sp-snmb piece (shipped) | Today | After convergence |
|---|---|---|
| `dedicated_fleet` column | Contract-only values (`"contract"` or `""`) | **Same column, same type, zero migration.** Becomes multi-valued: `"contract"`, `"manufacturing"`, `"factory"`, `"trade_route"`, `"gas"`, or operator-chosen sub-fleet names (`"bulk_circuit"`). |
| `reconcileDedicatedFleet` (write path, driven by the `--dedicated-ships` CLI flag at coordinator startup) | Direct field mutation via `shipRepo.Save`, additive-only (no unassign), **the only setter that exists today** | **Demoted to a thin bootstrap wrapper.** At startup, for each configured symbol it calls the same `AssignShipFleetCommand{ShipSymbol, Fleet: "contract"}` that the new `fleet assign` CLI calls (section 7) — not a second write path. The direct-`Save` mutation is deleted, not kept alongside. |
| `FindIdleLightHaulers`'s `DedicatedFleet() != ""` skip (discovery-filter) | **The only enforcement that exists today** | **Unchanged, kept permanently — this is layer 1 of 2.** Cheap, read-side, shared by every caller "for free," no lock needed. It stays exactly as a fast pre-filter: don't even attempt a claim on a hull you can already see is spoken for. Layer 2 is the new atomic check inside `ClaimShip` (section 4) — the authoritative guarantee, closing the TOCTOU between a discovery read and a claim write. **Both layers coexist deliberately**: layer 1 is an optimization, layer 2 is the correctness guarantee; dropping either regresses something (drop layer 1 → every coordinator wastes cycles attempting doomed claims; drop layer 2 → the TOCTOU reopens). |
| `FindIdleDedicatedShips(ctx, playerID, shipRepo, dedicatedShips []string)` — contract's own symbol-list lookup; comment notes the caller-supplied list itself *is* the authorization, not a `DedicatedFleet()` check | Only contract has this; the list is whatever symbols the coordinator remembers from its own `--dedicated-ships` startup flag | **Replaced by `FindIdleShipsByFleet(ctx, playerID, shipRepo, fleet string)`** — filters `allShips` where `ship.DedicatedFleet() == fleet`, the mirror-image of `FindIdleLightHaulers`'s exclude filter. This is not additive — the symbol-list variant is deleted. Reason it can't just stay alongside: once `fleet assign`/`unassign` (section 7) can change dedication at any time, a coordinator's *remembered* symbol list goes stale the moment the captain reassigns a ship without restarting that coordinator. Reading `DedicatedFleet()` from the DB on every discovery pass is what makes reassignment actually live instead of "live after next restart." |
| `HomeShipHandler` (standby homing) | Contract-owned by package location only; its logic is already fleet-agnostic (`ShipSymbol` + `StandbyStations []string` in, stateless navigate-to-nearest, no contract-specific code in the body) | Moves to a neutral package; invoked by fleet name. Standby stations stop being a parameter the contract coordinator passes from its own CLI flags and become a property of the fleet itself (decision in section 10) — `HomeShipHandler` looks them up by fleet name instead of receiving them per-call. |

Net effect: after convergence there is exactly **one** column, **one**
writer (`AssignShipFleetCommand`), **one** read-side pre-filter
(`FindIdleLightHaulers`'s exclude check, unchanged), **one** "give me my
fleet's idle ships" query (`FindIdleShipsByFleet`, generalized), and **one**
homing handler. sp-snmb's contract behavior is preserved bit-for-bit from the
operator's point of view — same flag, same effect — it just stops being a
separate code path underneath.

## 4. Ownership model — the converged mechanism

**The write side**: a new CQRS command, `AssignShipFleetCommand` /
`AssignShipFleetHandler`, modeled 1:1 on the already-shipped sp-i1ku pattern
(`ReserveShipCommand` → `ReserveShipHandler` →
`ShipRepository.ReserveForCaptain`, same row-locking transaction shape).
`AssignShipFleetCommand{ShipSymbol, Fleet, PlayerID}` where `Fleet == ""`
clears dedication — this is what closes the additive-only gap in
`reconcileDedicatedFleet` (section 3) and is the real captain command from
requirement #4 (section 7). This is the **only** legitimate way to change
`dedicated_fleet` after convergence.

**The enforcement side**: `ClaimShip`'s signature grows one parameter —
`ClaimShip(ctx, shipSymbol, containerID, playerID, operation string) error`
— and its existing locked transaction grows one more branch, symmetric to
the `ShipReservedByCaptainError` check already there: if
`model.DedicatedFleet != "" && model.DedicatedFleet != operation`, return a
new `ShipDedicatedToOtherFleetError`. This is deliberately kept as a
*second* command from the assignment write, not fused into it — "who
permanently owns this hull" (assign) and "who holds it right now" (claim)
are different concerns, and fusing them would force every reallocation to
also force-claim the ship immediately, which isn't always wanted (e.g., let
the current job finish, new owner claims it next cycle).

**Why not just extend `ClaimShip` alone (no new command)?** That was
considered and rejected: it closes the enforcement gap but gives the captain
no way to *set* the tag, so requirement #4 still needs something — and
whatever that "something" is would end up being this same command. Building
both together, rather than deferring the command, is what makes this a
convergence instead of a second later patch.

**Why not keep `reconcileDedicatedFleet`'s direct-mutation approach and just
call it from more coordinators?** Rejected — perpetuates the additive-only
bug, keeps the write un-atomic (`Save`, not a locked transaction — two
operators reconfiguring dedication concurrently can race each other), and
gives the captain no CLI verb. Doesn't satisfy requirement #4 and is exactly
the "parallel mechanism" this revision is required to avoid.

No schema migration required — `dedicated_fleet` is already free-text
(section 3). Namespace stays a flat string, not a structured
`{operation, sub_fleet}` type (decision in section 10).

Implementation note to flag for whoever builds this: `ClaimShip` already
calls `r.shipListCache.Delete(playerID.Value())` after its write — there's
an in-memory cache in front of the repo. `AssignShipFleetHandler` must
invalidate the same cache after writing the tag, or a freshly-dedicated ship
can still show up in another coordinator's discovery for one stale-cache
window.

## 5. Cargo-fit / type-aware hull selection

Today, hull selection inside an operation is nearest-only
(`task_assignment_manager.go`'s `m.shipSelector.FindClosestShip`) — no
capacity check at all. This directly produces two of the cited symptoms:
frigates multi-tripping contracts, and the heavy freighter dumping 225 units
into a negative-margin vol-20 sink (the only hull *discovered* was grossly
oversized for the lot, and nothing in selection prefers a right-sized hull
over an oversized one).

**Recommended shared policy** (generalizes the Admiral's contract-specific
ruling into one reusable function, e.g.
`ship/selection.SelectHullForCargo(candidates []*Ship, unitsNeeded int, from Waypoint) (*Ship, Mode, error)`):

1. Filter candidates to role `HAULER`.
2. Among haulers, keep only those whose `CargoCapacity() >= unitsNeeded`
   (single-trip fit). If any exist, return the **nearest one among the
   fitting set** — never the nearest overall, so a right-sized hull always
   beats an oversized one that happens to be one hop closer.
3. If no hauler fits in one trip, return the nearest hauler regardless of
   capacity, tagged `Mode = MultiTrip` — an explicit, logged multi-trip
   decision, not a silent frigate substitution.
4. Only if **zero haulers are idle at all** (not merely zero that fit) does
   the command ship/frigate become eligible, and only if the caller opted in
   (mirrors today's `CommandShipPolicy`) — last resort, never a first choice.
5. If nothing qualifies, return `NoHullAvailable` and let the caller decide
   to wait or escalate.

This is a pure function over already-loaded candidates, so it drops into
contract's existing selection point today and into mfg's
`task_assignment_manager.go` / trade-route's circuit-fill logic / the heavy
freighter's dispatch with no repository changes. Package placement: **not**
inside `contract` (mfg/trade-route importing from `contract` would be a
layering smell) — lives in a neutral package, e.g.
`internal/application/ship/selection`, that `contract` also imports going
forward, deleting the contract-local copy.

## 6. Standby / pre-positioning

Converges per section 3's table, corrected from rev 2: `HomeShipHandler`'s
*destination-selection* logic is **not** staying untouched — see "Balanced
hub assignment" below, which replaces its one `shared.FindNearestWaypoint`
call. What's still a pure generalization, as originally stated: everything
else in the handler's body (stateless, no temporary container/assignment
ceremony, no contract-specific code) — only a package move and a call-site
change, becoming invokable by any coordinator with idle dedicated ships, by
fleet name.

**Decision (section 10): standby stations are a property of the fleet, not
a per-coordinator flag.** Defined once per fleet name and looked up by
`HomeShipHandler` at homing time, instead of every coordinator re-declaring
its own `--standby-stations` list. This is the one piece of this design that
isn't a pure generalization of existing code — it implies a small
fleet-config record (fleet name → standby stations, and a natural home for
future fleet-level settings) alongside the per-ship `dedicated_fleet` tag.
Sized deliberately small: a lookup keyed by fleet name, not a new subsystem.

**Invariant: continuous re-homing, every cycle, not just at startup.** This
is not new behavior to build — it's already live in
`run_fleet_coordinator.go`'s per-task ship-selection loop (~lines 356-375):
whenever the loop rotates from one selected ship to a different one, the
*previous* ship is homed if it's dedicated (fire-and-forget `HomeShipCommand`
dispatch) — on every rotation, for the lifetime of the coordinator process,
not a one-shot call at boot. The generalization in this section must
preserve that shape as a **guaranteed invariant**, stated explicitly so
Phase 3 can't regress it by accident: *any idle-and-waiting dedicated
hauler is re-homed every cycle it goes idle, for as long as the coordinator
runs, using whichever event that coordinator's own loop already uses to
detect "this ship just freed up."* The trigger event's exact shape differs
per coordinator (contract's is `previousShipSymbol != selectedShip` inside
one rotating loop; other coordinators may detect idleness differently) —
Phase 3's coordinator-by-coordinator rollout (section 9) is exactly where
each coordinator's own "ship just went idle" event gets wired to the shared
homing call. What must not vary is the guarantee itself: homing is
re-evaluated every time, never cached from the last decision or run once at
startup.

**Balanced hub assignment (replaces `FindNearestWaypoint`).** Today's
`HomeShipHandler` (sp-snmb) sends every idle dedicated hauler to the single
nearest standby station via `shared.FindNearestWaypoint`. Observed in
production: this clumps the fleet — e.g. 3 haulers parked at J56, none near
H49/B7 — because "nearest" is evaluated per-ship with no knowledge of where
the *other* idle haulers already are or are headed. A hauler homed to a
station nobody needs covered isn't pre-positioned, it's just parked.

Precedent already exists in this codebase for capacity-aware, non-greedy
distribution — this policy adapts rather than invents:
- `domain/contract.FleetAssigner.AssignShipsToTargets` already distributes a
  ship list across target waypoints with a per-target cap
  (`MaxShipsPerMarket`) and nearest-available-with-remaining-capacity
  selection, specifically to avoid over-concentration.
- `BalanceShipPositionHandler`'s `ShipBalancer.SelectOptimalBalancingPosition`
  already scores candidate markets by a "Distance + Coverage Score" for the
  *non*-dedicated hauler case, taking the rest of the idle-hauler pool into
  account rather than scoring each ship in isolation.

Neither existing mechanism weights by contract value — both are pure
distance/capacity balancers. The new policy for dedicated standby hubs:

1. Each standby station carries a **value weight** — the trade-analyst crew
   role's per-hub contract-value ranking. This design treats the ranking as
   an external input it consumes, not a Go ranking engine it builds; the
   fleet-config record from the decision above is the natural home to store
   it (e.g. station entries become `{waypoint, weight}` instead of a bare
   symbol list).
2. A station counts as **covered** if a dedicated hauler is already sitting
   there or already homing there — both count, which is what stops two
   haulers converging on the same already-covered hub in the same cycle.
3. When a dedicated hauler goes idle: assign it to the **highest-weight
   currently-uncovered** standby station in its fleet's list. Break ties by
   travel distance (cheapest reposition among equally-valuable uncovered
   options).
4. If every standby station is already covered, fall back to nearest
   station overall (today's behavior) — a second hauler stacking onto an
   already-covered high-value hub still beats parking at a low-value one
   for the sake of being "uncovered."
5. Rebalancing is implicit, not a separate scheduled pass: because step 3
   runs on every idle-transition (per the continuous re-homing invariant
   above), the assignment recomputes fresh every time a hauler frees up, or
   a previously-homed hauler gets dispatched away and un-covers its station
   for the next idle hauler to claim.

This explicitly **replaces** the `shared.FindNearestWaypoint` call in
`HomeShipHandler` (today at `home_ship.go:85`) with a coverage-weighted
selection over the fleet's standby list plus the current
position/in-flight-destination of sibling dedicated ships — the handler
needs read visibility into the rest of its fleet, which it doesn't today
(today's call is single-ship, stateless). That's a real, if small, increase
in what the handler reads — it still costs no container/assignment
ceremony (per section 3), just a wider read before the same single
navigate-if-needed write.

## 7. Captain re-allocation command

**Decision (section 10): one `fleet` command group**, not folded into
`ship`:

```
spacetraders fleet assign   --ship TORWIND-19 --fleet bulk_circuit
spacetraders fleet unassign --ship TORWIND-19          # clears dedication (Fleet: "")
spacetraders fleet list                                 # every fleet name, its members, its standby stations
```

Precedent for the plumbing: sp-i1ku's `ship reserve`/`ship release` pair —
CLI → gRPC daemon-client call → `AssignShipFleetCommand` →
`AssignShipFleetHandler` → repository, persisted, restart-safe. `fleet
assign`/`unassign` follow the identical shape, just a new command group and
a new handler instead of reusing `ReserveShip`'s.

This supersedes `reconcileDedicatedFleet`'s coordinator-flag-only mutation
as the *only* sanctioned way to change dedication (section 3) — the
`--dedicated-ships` startup flag becomes a convenience wrapper that calls
`fleet assign` once per symbol at boot, not a separate path.

## 8. Restart-survival

Already satisfied for the static tag today — `dedicated_fleet` is a
persisted `ship_models` column, not in-memory state, so it survives a
restart regardless of which coordinator set it. The new fleet-config record
(section 6, standby stations) needs the same treatment: a persisted table,
not coordinator-local memory, or standby homing would silently lose its
target list on every restart.

The gap is on the *dynamic claim* side for the two non-atomic paths
(factory, trade-route, gas): their `Save`-based claims persist fine, so a
restart doesn't lose the assignment record itself — the risk is concurrent
corruption *before* a restart, not data loss *at* restart (see section 9,
Phase 2). `ReleaseAllActive`'s existing restart-reconciliation pass already
correctly excludes captain reservations by `assignment_owner`; it needs no
change for dedication, since dedication and active-assignment are already
orthogonal fields.

## 9. Migration plan (phased, so contract's income-critical path is never blocked)

**Phase 1 — converge, don't parallel-build:**
- Add the `ShipDedicatedToOtherFleetError` branch inside `ClaimShip`'s
  existing transaction (section 4).
- Ship `AssignShipFleetCommand` + `AssignShipFleetHandler` + the `fleet`
  CLI/gRPC surface (sections 4, 7).
- **Delete `reconcileDedicatedFleet`'s direct-`Save` mutation** and replace
  it with calls to `AssignShipFleetCommand` — this is the specific change
  that prevents two live write paths to the same column existing at once.
- **Delete the symbol-list `FindIdleDedicatedShips`** and replace contract's
  call site with the generalized `FindIdleShipsByFleet(ctx, playerID,
  shipRepo, "contract")` — this is the specific change that prevents two
  live "find my dedicated ships" queries existing at once.
- `FindIdleLightHaulers`'s exclude filter is untouched (section 3) —
  nothing to converge there, it was already correct and shared.

Net: contract's operator-facing behavior (`--dedicated-ships` flag) is
bit-for-bit unchanged; underneath, there is exactly one setter and one
fleet-scoped query, not one-per-coordinator. Manufacturing (already on
`ClaimShip`) gets TOCTOU-closed for free the moment Phase 1 ships, with zero
changes to manufacturing's own code.

**Phase 2 — migrate the two non-atomic claim paths onto `ClaimShip`:**
Factory's `claimShipForFactory` and trade-route/gas's raw
`FindBySymbol`→mutate→`Save` calls switch to
`ClaimShip(ctx, symbol, containerID, playerID, operation)`. This closes
Bug A's remaining exposure and the read-modify-write race itself (today's
weakest link, weaker than the dedication gap) — not just adds
dedication-awareness to these three coordinators. Each coordinator gets a
constant `operation` identity (`"factory"`, `"trade_route"`, `"gas"`).

**Phase 3 — behavioral generalization:**
Wire in the shared `SelectHullForCargo` policy (section 5) and the
generalized `HomeShipHandler` + fleet-config standby stations (section 6)
coordinator-by-coordinator, lowest-risk first (trade-route's circuit-fill
and the heavy freighter's dispatch are the two symptoms explicitly cited,
so they lead). This is also where `HomeShipHandler`'s destination selection
flips from nearest-only to the balanced hub assignment policy (section 6):
the `shared.FindNearestWaypoint` call at `home_ship.go:85` is replaced
outright, not kept as a parallel path — the policy's own step 4 already
degrades to nearest-overall once every standby station is covered, so no
second code path is needed alongside it. Each coordinator picks up hull
selection and balanced homing together in the same migration step, since
both are changes to the same generalized call site it's adopting.

Phase 1 is small, additive to every caller except the two sp-snmb functions
it explicitly retires, and directly addresses the income-critical contract
line with the least churn — recommend starting there regardless of how the
rest of the phasing is sequenced.

## 10. Decisions locked for this revision (recommended, Admiral to confirm)

1. **Flat-string fleet namespace — keep flat.** sp-snmb already ships this
   (`"contract"` as a bare string); a structured `{operation, sub_fleet}`
   type would be new schema and code for a distinction the fleet doesn't
   need yet — one flat string already carries sub-fleet names like
   `"bulk_circuit"` fine. Recommended, Admiral to confirm.
2. **Standby stations are a property of the fleet**, not a per-coordinator
   flag (section 6). Defined once per fleet name, looked up by
   `HomeShipHandler` at homing time. Avoids every coordinator re-declaring
   the same station list and keeps homing config attached to the ownership
   tag it travels with. This is the one real scope increase in the design
   (a small fleet-config lookup) — still recommended over the duplication
   alternative. Recommended, Admiral to confirm.
3. **Command surface is one `fleet assign/unassign/list` group** (section
   7), not folded into `ship`. Keeps the ownership-tag verbs together and
   distinct from `ship reserve/release`'s captain-manual-use semantics,
   which is a different mechanism (dynamic personal claim, not static
   dedication) that shouldn't be confused with this one. Recommended,
   Admiral to confirm.

Produce ONE coherent design on this bead, then captain+Admiral review BEFORE building. THE PROBLEM (one root cause, many symptoms): the fleet has no ownership model — every coordinator (contracts/factories/mfg/trade-route) grabs any idle-or-nearest hull by a dumb LOCAL rule that ignores net income. Symptoms observed today: contract coordinator selects by DISTANCE-ONLY so the 40-cargo frigate multi-trips 47u contracts; factories/mfg POACH the light haulers contracts need (starved the best line, +9.5M/day, to near-zero for 4h); trade-route dumps a 225-cargo heavy into a vol-20 home sink (negative margin); reservations lose races / restart-safe daemons can't be cleanly stopped. DESIGN SHOULD COVER: (1) a persistent ship->operation ownership/dedication model (contract_fleet, bulk_circuit_fleet, factory_pool, scout_pool) that coordinators respect — no poaching; (2) cargo-fit + type-aware hull selection within an operation (haulers-first, frigate last-resort, hold>=units-in-one-trip); (3) standby/pre-positioning (idle dedicated haulers home to strategic contract-source waypoints {J56,E42/K80,H49,B7}); (4) how the captain re-allocates hulls between pools cleanly (a real command, not fragile reservations); (5) how dedicated hulls survive daemon restarts. Deliver: approach options + tradeoffs + recommended architecture. This is the structural fix that ends the whole class of chaos.

title: contract cycle-time optimizer: NEVER skip contracts (Admiral ruling) — right-size hull + instant chaining to clear micro-contracts fastest
description: Post-qpmi ledger shows fulfilled contracts at 2,500 and 7,840cr alongside 104k-287k ones (e.g. cmrcy4l51 2,500 vs cmrcxtdbr 142,340, both ~03:2xZ). A cycle consumes the same ship-hours regardless of payout — negotiate-on-delivery must COMPARE and REJECT/skip below a floor (e.g. floor = rolling-median-payout x 0.3, or opportunity-cost per ship-hour from duty-cycle KPI). Verify first in code whether negotiation offers a choice or is take-it-or-leave-it (if forced, the floor becomes 'abandon cheap contracts early when a better one is negotiable'). Acceptance: no fulfilled contract below floor in a 24h window; contract $/ship-hour rises vs tonight's baseline.

ADMIRAL RULING (2026-07-09 ~04:15Z): contracts can NEVER be skipped/rejected — accept and fulfill ALL, fastest possible. Value-floor premise WITHDRAWN. Reframed spec: micro-contracts are GATES between contracts (1-active API cap) — every minute they linger delays the next negotiation. Optimize TIME-TO-CLEAR: (1) HULL RIGHT-SIZING — assign the FASTEST adequate hull, not the default hauler: a 2-unit delivery on the speed-36 command frigate clears ~2.4x faster than a speed-15 hauler; reserve 80-cargo hulls for contracts that need the hold; (2) instant negotiate-on-delivery (qpmi, live) stays; (3) PRE-POSITION: while a contract is in its delivery leg, position the next-likely fulfillment hull at the probable source market (cached export data predicts it); (4) parallel sourcing when contract quantity spans multiple trips — split across hulls. ACCEPTANCE: median time-to-clear for sub-10k contracts drops >40%; contract chain throughput (contracts/day) rises; zero skipped/expired contracts.
REVERTED value-floor impl 9e79eda — it implemented the WITHDRAWN reject-below-floor approach, violating the Admiral's 'NEVER skip contracts / zero skipped' ruling (and it idles ships on rejected contracts via error 4511 resurfacing — opposite of the goal). Correct spec = CYCLE-TIME OPTIMIZER (hull right-sizing, pre-position, split quantities, keep qpmi chaining; ZERO skips). Rebuild on shipwright/sp-snmb-v2.
ADMIRAL HULL RULING (05:1xZ): NO frigate purchases — frigate class is capped by small cargo (60) AND small fuel holds; refuel stops (dock+refuel+orbit + detours) eat the speed-36 edge, worst on long/cross-gate legs at BURN burn rates. Right-size the contract chain with OWNED hulls only (T1 frigate for small fast legs where its tank suffices, lights for the rest). The C18D 905k frigate option is DEAD. Heavy frames (speed+hold+tank together) remain the only purchase class in favor — TORWIND-19's realized loop stays the template.
ADMIRAL ADDITION (05:2xZ) — element 5, DISTRIBUTED STANDBY NET: idle haulers HOME to assigned standby stations (top contract-source markets) instead of parking where they finished. Next contract → nearest standby hull claims → travel-to-source ~0 for most contracts (cycle time is ~2/3 travel; source leg is the killable half of it). Implementation: idle-homing policy in the pool (on release, if no claim within N sec, navigate to assigned station; claimable mid-homing); station assignments from contract HISTORY — analyst to rank top 4-6 source markets by frequency across the 380-contract corpus (likely D39/D40/K80/J56/E41 cluster); re-rank weekly-equivalent. Synergy: standby stations double as factory feed/lift points — a homed hull is also positioned for factory claims (the pool stays fungible). ACCEPTANCE addition: median travel-to-source leg across contracts drops >50%; standby coverage = a hull within 1 hop of the source for >70% of new contracts.
TRADE-ANALYST DATA (sp-snmb element 5: contract source-market ranking for standby stations) — 2026-07-09 ~05:25Z
Method: all 382 fulfilled era-2 contracts linked to their actual PURCHASE waypoint via ledger (contract good + accept→fulfill window). Recency weight: half-life 24h.

**RANKED STATION LIST (frequency × recency, home system):**
1. **J56** — 44 contracts, 11.1% weighted share (also: DRUGS export, our clothing/medicine sink; far NW at (-588,416))
2. **K80** — 42, 10.5% (clothing export; central at (-58,89))
3. **H49** — 40, 10.3% (refinery: iron/copper/aluminum source; central at (46,-5))
4. **C37** — 38, 10.3% (central-north (36,152))
5. **B7** — 33, 9.1% (exchange; south at (-168,-300))
6. **E42** — 31, 8.6% (machinery/fabrics export; near-centroid (-28,46))
(next: D39 6.7%, G48 5.6%, E43 5.5%, H51 4.7%)

**KEY STRUCTURAL FINDING: the distribution is FLAT** — no market exceeds ~11%; exact-match coverage of even the top-6 is only 60%. Camping any single market is pointless; a NET is the right shape (validates the Admiral's concept). Geography rescues it: sources CLUSTER.

**COVERAGE (share of contracts with source within 80 dist-units of a station — ~2.5 min one-way at spd 15, ~1 min at spd 36):**
- Top-4 net {J56, K80, H49, C37}: **83.2%** weighted
- Top-6 net {+B7, E42}: **92.3%** weighted
- Exact-match only (standby AT the source): top-4 42%, top-6 60% — the 80-unit adjacency is what makes the net work.

**Placement advice:** K80/H49/C37/E42 form one central cluster (~150-unit box near origin) — E42 is the near-centroid; a hauler parked there reaches all four in ≤~2 min. So an efficient 4-net is: **J56 (far NW anchor) + E42-or-K80 (central) + H49 (central-south) + B7 (south)** — same ~83% coverage with better spread than the raw top-4. 5th/6th stations: C37 and D39. Note J56 is BOTH the #1 source and the most isolated — its standby slot is the highest-value single assignment (11% of sources, nothing else within 80 units... its nearest ranked neighbor is far).

**Caveats:** mix drifts with contract RNG — recency weighting damps this but re-run this ranking daily (method is 1 query + script, on the bead's parent thread). Source choice is endogenous: ships buy where the coordinator routes them (cheapest/closest), so these are 'revealed-optimal' sources under current routing, which is exactly what standby placement should mirror. Confidence HIGH on ranking/coverage arithmetic (full population, not sampled); MEDIUM on the 80-unit adjacency threshold (chosen as ~2.5min hop; tune to the coordinator's claim-timeout).

CAPTAIN ACCEPT (05:3xZ): standby net v1 = the 4-station set {J56, E42/K80, H49, B7} @83% coverage within ~2.5min hop; phase J56 first (top source + isolated). 6-net (92%) only if the marginal 2 stations don't thin factory-claim availability. Implementation stays engine-side (idle-homing, this bead) behind wlev+vsfn — no manual pre-positioning (fights the pool, monitors-between-wakes anti-pattern).
